# TimeSformer (Kinetics-400) - Comparative Analysis: MI300X vs H100

## Executive Summary

TimeSformer base (divided space-time attention, ViT-based video model) was profiled with BF16 precision on MI300X (Trace 1) and H100 (Trace 2). The MI300X trace completes in 121.27 ms vs 160.00 ms on H100, making MI300X 24.2% faster overall. Both traces show high GPU compute utilization (>96%) with negligible idle time and no collective communication. The dominant optimization opportunity on MI300X is in GEMM operations — specifically two groups of memory-bound batched matrix multiplies and compute-bound linear projections that run slower on MI300X than H100, accounting for a combined ~13.97% potential end-to-end improvement.

| Metric | Trace 1 - (MI300X) | Trace 2 - (H100) | Difference |
|--------|----------------------------|-------------------------------|------------|
| Total Time | 121.27 ms | 160.00 ms | -38.73 ms (-24.2%) |
| Compute % | 97.93% | 96.86% | -1.07% |
| Idle % | 1.17% | 0.97% | +0.20% |
| Exposed Communication % | 0.00% | 0.00% | 0.00% |
| Top Bottleneck Category | GEMM (44.65%) | Other (45.36%) | — |

![Performance Improvement](data:image/png;base64,)

## Compute Kernel Optimizations

Findings from per-category kernel analysis (GEMM, elementwise, normalization, convolution, reduce, other).
Summaries of recommendations from compute kernel sub-agents, focused on individual kernel efficiency.

### Top Operations

<!-- impact-begin kind=top_ops -->
| Rank | Category | Trace 1 Time (ms) | Trace 2 Time (ms) | % of Compute Time | Ops | Difference (ms) | Potential improvement (time, E2E %) |
|------|----------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-----|-----------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | GEMM | 54.144 | 53.568 | 45.60% | 11 | -0.576 | ~14.5–19.4 ms (11.97–15.97%) | <!-- top-ops-row low=11.97 high=15.97 -->
| 2 | Other | 43.396 | 71.871 | 36.55% | 8 | +28.475 | — |
| 3 | elementwise | 19.532 | 29.429 | 16.45% | 18 | +9.897 | — |
<!-- impact-end -->

### 🔴 P1: Memory-Bound Tiny Batched BMM Kernels (Tensile)

**Insight**: Two `aten::bmm` shapes with batch size 37,632 and small inner dimensions (M=8, N=8/64) run dramatically slower on MI300X, with the dominant variant 96% slower than H100.

**Action**: Optimize memory access patterns for these tiny-dimension batched GEMMs. Profile the dominant member kernels with hardware counters to diagnose occupancy and tiling issues for the extremely low arithmetic intensity (3.76 FLOPS/Byte).

<!-- impact-begin kind=p_item category=gemm low=9.45 mid=11.02 high=12.6 -->
**Impact**: impact_score: 11.02
<!-- impact-end -->

→ *See [Detailed Analysis: Compute kernel insights > P1](#detailed-analysis-compute-p1) for details*

---

### 🟡 P2: Compute-Bound Linear Projection Kernels (Tensile)

**Insight**: Six `aten::addmm` projection operations with large M (~25K) run 27–36% slower on MI300X than on H100.

**Action**: Profile the dominant member kernels for tile-size and wave-occupancy tuning. Consider narrowing precision from BF16 to FP8 if the model tolerates it to reduce the compute floor.

<!-- impact-begin kind=p_item category=gemm low=2.52 mid=2.95 high=3.37 -->
**Impact**: impact_score: 2.95
<!-- impact-end -->

→ *See [Detailed Analysis: Compute kernel insights > P2](#detailed-analysis-compute-p2) for details*

---

## Kernel Fusion Opportunities (Experimental)

> **Note:** Kernel fusion analysis is experimental.

No kernel fusion opportunities detected.

---

## System-Level Optimizations

> **Note:** System-level analysis is exploratory. The patterns and recommendations below are under active development and may be refined as system-level analysis matures.

> **Comparative note:** System-level analysis is performed on the primary trace (Trace 1) only. Cross-trace system-level comparison is not yet supported.

Findings from system-level analysis (GPU utilization, memory transfer patterns,
communication/compute overlap). These affect the GPU pipeline as a whole.

✅ No system-level bottlenecks detected. GPU activity breakdown shows 97.93% computation, with 0.90% memcpy and negligible communication overhead. Memory copy consists of a single H2D transfer (36.8 MB, 1.09 ms) and one small D2H transfer (800 B, 0.004 ms), both below flagging thresholds. No collective communication events were observed.

---

## Detailed Analysis

### Compute Kernel Insights

<a id="detailed-analysis-compute-p1"></a>
#### 🔴 P1: Memory-Bound Tiny Batched BMM Kernels (Tensile)

**Identification:** Two `aten::bmm` operations with batch size 37,632 and small inner matrix dimensions (M=8, N=8 or N=64) were flagged because Trace 1 is substantially slower than Trace 2. These operations use the **Tensile** backend. (source: `gemm_metrics.json` → `category_findings[0].members[].efficiency_pct` < 100, `operations[].library`)

**Data:**

| Operation | Trace 1 Time (ms) | Trace 2 Time (ms) | Count (T1/T2) | Difference (ms) | FLOPS/Byte (T1) | Bound (T1) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
| aten::bmm (37632,8,64) | 13.710 | 0.490 | 12 / 12 | -13.220 | 3.76 | memory-bound |
| aten::bmm (37632,8,8) | 3.356 | 1.301 | 12 / 12 | -2.056 | 3.76 | memory-bound |

**Reasoning for Slowdown:** Both shapes are memory-bound (FLOPS/Byte = 3.76), so throughput is limited by HBM bandwidth. With tiny M=8 and K/N of 8 or 64 spread across 37,632 batch elements, each individual GEMM moves very little data, and the kernel must rely on coalescing accesses across the batch dimension to sustain HBM bandwidth. Poor coalescing results in redundant HBM reads and low effective bandwidth — the dominant cause of high kernel time in Trace 1.

**Resolution:** Better memory access patterns reduce the bytes fetched per GEMM invocation, narrowing the throughput gap between Trace 1 and Trace 2. For these tiny inner-dimension shapes, a tiling strategy that coalesces global memory traffic across the large batch dimension avoids redundant HBM reads, directly improving kernel time.

**Impact estimate:**
<!-- impact-begin kind=detail_estimate low=9.45 high=12.6 -->
- Low end impact_score (75% gap target): 9.45
- High end impact_score (100% gap target): 12.6
<!-- impact-end -->

---

<a id="detailed-analysis-compute-p2"></a>
#### 🟡 P2: Compute-Bound Linear Projection Kernels (Tensile)

**Identification:** Six `aten::addmm` operations with M approximately 25,000 and K/N in the range 768–3072 were flagged because Trace 1 is consistently slower than Trace 2 across all variants. These operations use the **Tensile** backend. (source: `gemm_metrics.json` → `category_findings[1].members[].efficiency_pct` < 100, `operations[].library`)

**Data:**

| Operation | Trace 1 Time (ms) | Trace 2 Time (ms) | Count (T1/T2) | Difference (ms) | FLOPS/Byte (T1) | Bound (T1) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
| aten::addmm (25089,768→3072) | 3.057 | 1.978 | 12 / 12 | -1.080 | 600.09 | compute-bound |
| aten::addmm (25089,3072→768) | 2.601 | 1.905 | 12 / 12 | -0.696 | 599.81 | compute-bound |
| aten::addmm (25096,768→2304) | 2.310 | 1.512 | 12 / 12 | -0.798 | 563.43 | compute-bound |
| aten::addmm (25088,768→2304) | 2.241 | 1.479 | 12 / 12 | -0.762 | 563.42 | compute-bound |
| aten::addmm (25088,768→768) | 1.706 | 1.125 | 24 / 24 | -0.581 | 378.45 | compute-bound |
| aten::addmm (25096,768→768) | 0.891 | 0.568 | 12 / 12 | -0.323 | 378.45 | compute-bound |

**Reasoning for Slowdown:** Trace 2 runs these six projections 27–36% faster than Trace 1, with per-op time gaps ranging from 0.32ms to 1.08ms. The gap is consistent across all six shapes, pointing to the Tensile backend's kernel selection for these M/K/N dimensions rather than a workload-specific anomaly. Operations likely use suboptimal tile sizes that reduce wave count per CU, leaving compute units underutilized.

**Resolution:** Tuning tile sizes increases compute unit utilization per wave, bringing throughput closer to hardware peak for these large M projections. If the model can tolerate reduced precision (e.g. FP8 instead of BF16), halving the bytes moved per invocation directly reduces the compute floor, which can close or eliminate the time gap observed against Trace 2.

**Impact estimate:**
<!-- impact-begin kind=detail_estimate low=2.52 high=3.37 -->
- Low end impact_score (75% gap target): 2.52
- High end impact_score (100% gap target): 3.37
<!-- impact-end -->

### Kernel Fusion Insights

> **Note:** Kernel fusion analysis is experimental.

No fusion impact estimates available.

### System-Level Insights

No system-level issues detected. GPU activity is 97.93% compute with a single H2D transfer (1.09 ms) and no collective communication.

## Appendix

### Model Architecture
- **Model**: TimeSformer (Kinetics-400)
- **Architecture**: Transformer (divided space-time attention, ViT-based video)
- **Scale**: base (hidden=768, heads=12, patch=16, 8 frames, 224x224)
- **Precision**: BF16

### Hardware Reference

- **Platform (Trace 1)**: MI300X
- **Peak HBM BW**: 5.3 TB/s
- **Peak MAF (BF16)**: 708 TFLOPS
- **Peak MAF (FP8)**: 1273 TFLOPS
